title: Tiny House Loft Bedroom System
summary: A built-in queen bed with drawer storage under a walk-in loft floor above, framed off a 4x4 wall system with a hidden storage door — genuinely a whole-room build, not a single piece of furniture.
description:
This is a built-in system, not a standalone piece: a 4x4 post-and-wall structure carries both a queen bed platform with drawer storage below and a full loft floor above, framed to span the room and rest on wall cleats. **The exact post heights, loft-floor span, and wall-cleat length here are this build's own room** — the source is explicit that the wall cleat length matched their own 94-1/2" room width, so treat every long-span dimension as a worked example to re-measure against your own space, not a number to cut blind.

Build the 4x4 wall frame first, using pocket holes hidden on the underside where they attach into the posts, then build the 2x8 bed support square before anything else — take extra care here, since an out-of-square bed frame is what causes drawers built into the cavity below to bind on their slides. **The 2x8 joists were chosen specifically to leave room for drawers underneath** — 2x4 or 2x6 framing would work fine if you're skipping the drawers.

Every drawer and the tilt-out shoe storage are built to fit their own openings and slides, not the plan's stated numbers, with an even 1/8" gap intended around every door and drawer face once installed. The loft floor above uses 2x6 tongue-and-groove decking, chosen because the span crosses a doorway close to 36" wide — thinner 1x material is explicitly not recommended for that gap. **Store-bought black iron pipe stands in for the ladder and railing** — the source flags this isn't code-approved hardware, so build and use it with that in mind rather than treating it as a certified guardrail system.

materials.name: 2x6 tongue and groove, for loft floor/ceiling
1/4" plywood, full sheet (drawer bottoms, shoe storage backs)
Beadboard, paneling, or 1x6 T&G, full sheet (wall back)
Bottom side mount drawer slides, 24 in, pair
Black iron pipe, for ladder and railing
Primer and paint, or stain and poly

cutList.material: 2x6 tongue and groove, for loft floor/ceiling
cutList.note: Enough T&G to cover the full loft floor area; quantity depends on your loft's footprint.

steps.title: Measure your room, then cut the parts
steps.body: Read every long-span number here as a worked example from the source's own room, not a cut-and-go dimension: the room-length wall cleat is listed at 94-1/2" because that was their room's width, and the 52" and 71" wall posts were cut to their ceiling and loft heights. Measure your own wall-to-wall span and ceiling height first and substitute. Then cut: from the two 6 ft 4x4s, one short wall post at 52" and one tall at 71". From the fourteen 2x4s: four wall studs at 53", two short ladder supports at 13", one ladder support at 8", two bed side rails at 80", four bed legs at 16-1/2", wall cleats at 76", 80" and your room length, one short loft joist at 60", and four loft joists at 82". Those fill all fourteen boards with nothing to spare, and the room-length cleat needs a full 8 ft board to itself, so cut the long pieces before the short ones. From the 2x8s: the 80" bed front rail off the 8 ft board, and four 58-1/2" bed joists off the two 10 ft boards, two per board. From the three 1x6s, six drawer sides at 24", four drawer front/backs at 22" and two more at 22-1/2" for the third drawer. Cut the plywood drawer bottoms at 24" x 23-1/2" (two) and 24" x 24" (one), and leave the drawer and door faces until the openings exist.
materials: 1/4" plywood, full sheet (drawer bottoms, shoe storage backs)

steps.title: Build the wall system
steps.body: Build the 4x4 wall frame first — the short 52" post, the tall 71" post, and the four 53" 2x4 studs between them — since everything else in this system hangs off it. Drill the pocket holes so they land on the UNDERSIDE of the horizontal members where they enter the posts, which keeps every hole out of sight once the wall backing goes on. Use 2-1/2" pocket hole screws and glue, and check the frame for square and plumb against the actual wall before it is fastened, not after.

steps.title: Build the bed support and attach to the wall
steps.body: Assemble the 2x4 bed support from the two 80" side rails and the four 16-1/2" legs, and take real trouble over square here — this frame becomes the cavity the drawers run in, and an out-of-square opening is what makes a drawer bind on its slides halfway out. Fasten the frame to itself and into the room's wall studs with 2-3/4" self-tapping screws, and into the 4x4 posts with the 5" lag screws, predrilling every lag so the post does not split. Level the frame in both directions before the last fasteners go in.

steps.title: Attach the wall cleats
steps.body: The loft floor rests on these, so they carry real load: find the wall studs, mark them, and screw each cleat into the framing with 2-3/4" screws — never into drywall alone. Set each cleat with a level rather than by measuring down from a ceiling, since a ceiling is rarely level and a loft floor that follows one is worse than one that does not. Cut the longest cleat to your own room's measured length, not to the 94-1/2" in the cut list.

steps.title: Build the bed joists
steps.body: Frame the bed platform from the 2x8s — the 80" front rail and the four 58-1/2" joists — using 2-3/4" self-tapping screws through the rail into each joist end. The 7-1/4" depth of a 2x8 is a deliberate choice: it is what leaves a tall enough cavity underneath for the drawers. If you are skipping the drawers, 2x4 or 2x6 framing carries the same load and costs less. Space the joists evenly across the 80" rail and check the frame is square and flat before it takes any weight.

steps.title: Add the wall backing
steps.body: Cut and attach the wall backing panel behind the storage area with nails and glue.
materials: Beadboard, paneling, or 1x6 T&G, full sheet (wall back)

steps.title: Lay the loft floor
steps.body: Frame the loft floor from the four 82" joists and the 60" short joist, resting on the wall cleats you just set, then deck it in 2x6 tongue and groove. The thicker T&G is not a style choice — the floor crosses a doorway opening close to 36" wide with no support beneath it, and 1x material is explicitly not strong enough over that gap. Start each course tongue-out, tap the next board home with a scrap offcut and a hammer so you never strike the tongue itself, and blind-nail through the tongue with 1-1/4" finish nails so the fasteners disappear. Buy enough T&G for your loft's actual footprint; the single 96" row in the cut list is one board, not the whole floor.
materials: 2x6 tongue and groove, for loft floor/ceiling

steps.title: Build the drawers and shoe storage
steps.body: Measure each opening under the bed platform before cutting anything, and build each box to that opening less the clearance the 24" bottom-mount slides require. In each box the two 24" sides run the full depth and the front and back sit between them: the 22" front/backs give a 23-1/2" outside width matching the 23-1/2" bottom, and the third drawer's 22-1/2" front/backs give 24", matching its 24" bottom. Check that against your own opening before you assemble the second box. Glue and nail the 1/4" plywood bottoms on with 1-1/4" finish nails, checking each box square as the bottom goes on, since the bottom is what locks the box square. Box out the tilt-out shoe storage from 1x3 scrap with a plywood back, sized to its own opening.
materials: 1/4" plywood, full sheet (drawer bottoms, shoe storage backs)

steps.title: Attach the drawer and door faces
steps.body: Hang the drawer boxes on their slides and the doors on their hinges first, then fit the faces to what you actually have. The two 7" x 24-1/4" drawer faces, the 7" x 24-3/4" third face, the two 16-1/4" x 24-1/4" door panels and the 16-1/4" x 24-3/4" third panel are all cut from the 3/4" plywood. Shim each face with 1/8" spacers on all four sides, clamp it, then screw it from INSIDE the drawer box so no fastener shows on the front. Work across the run checking the gaps with the same spacer every time — an even 1/8" all round is what makes a built-in read as built-in.
materials: Bottom side mount drawer slides, 24 in, pair

steps.title: Add the bed slats and hidden storage door
steps.body: Lay the eight 80" slats across the bed joists and fasten them down, splicing any joint directly over a joist so no slat end is unsupported. Then hinge the 52-3/4" x 12-3/4" plywood hidden storage door over the front opening. Cut a finger pull into it rather than fitting a knob — a knob on a door at shin height in a tiny house is something you will kick. Check the door swings clear of the drawer faces below it before the hinges are final.
materials: 2x6 tongue and groove, for loft floor/ceiling

steps.title: Add the ladder and railing
steps.body: Assemble the black iron pipe into a ladder and railing and anchor it into the 2x4 ladder supports and the loft framing, not into paneling — the two 13" short supports and the 8" support exist to give those anchors something solid to land in. Predrill every fastener into the framing. Understand the tradeoff the source names openly: iron pipe is not code-approved guardrail hardware, so treat this as a fixture you have judged for your own household rather than a certified rail, and test it under load before anyone climbs it.
materials: Black iron pipe, for ladder and railing

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand all surfaces, apply primer and paint or stain and poly, and add the knobs or handles.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

images.alt: Tiny house loft bedroom system with built-in bed and storage
